learn.title: Pumped Hydro: The World's Largest Battery
learn.intro: As we shift to wind and solar, we need a way to store massive amounts of energy for when the sun sets or wind stops. Pumped hydro storage acts as a giant mechanical battery, stabilizing the grid by storing energy as gravitational potential, ensuring a steady power supply and preventing blackouts during peak demand periods.
learn.c1_text: Think of it like a giant rechargeable battery using water. When electricity is cheap, water is pumped from a lower reservoir to an upper one. When demand spikes, that water is released downward through turbines. It is like a pendulum that stores energy by lifting a weight to be released later.
learn.c2_text: The system converts electrical energy into potential energy. When water flows down, it spins turbines to generate electricity. This process is highly efficient, often recovering 70-80% of the energy used for pumping, making it the most cost-effective way to store gigawatt-hours of energy for long durations.
learn.c3_text: India is rapidly expanding its pumped storage capacity to support its 500 GW non-fossil fuel target. Projects in states like Maharashtra and Tamil Nadu are critical for balancing the grid as the country integrates massive solar parks, ensuring stability across the National Grid.
learn.stat1: Global Share: ~90% of all energy storage
learn.stat3: India target: 500 GW non-fossil capacity by 2030

learn.title: Solar PV and the Photoelectric Effect
learn.intro: Solar photovoltaic (PV) technology is the cornerstone of the global energy transition, turning sunlight directly into electricity. Understanding the physics behind it reveals how we can harvest an infinite resource to power everything from small calculators to entire cities without emitting greenhouse gases during operation.
learn.c1_text: Imagine sunlight as a stream of tiny energy packets called photons. When these photons hit a silicon cell, they act like billiard balls, knocking electrons loose from their atoms. This movement of electrons is exactly what creates an electrical current, turning light into usable power.
learn.c2_text: To keep electrons moving in one direction, cells use two layers of silicon (P-type and N-type). This creates an internal electric field, acting like a one-way street for electrons. This forced flow creates the Direct Current (DC) that we then convert to Alternating Current (AC) for home use.
learn.c3_text: India has become a global solar leader through the National Solar Mission. From the massive Bhadla Solar Park to rooftop installations, the plummeting cost of PV modules has made solar the cheapest source of new electricity generation in the country.

learn.title: Floating Solar Farms: Power on Water
learn.intro: Land is precious for agriculture and forestry, leading to the rise of 'floatovoltaics.' By placing solar panels on reservoirs and lakes, we solve land-use conflicts while simultaneously improving panel efficiency and protecting water resources, creating a symbiotic relationship between energy production and water conservation.
learn.c1_text: Solar panels lose efficiency as they get hot. Water acts like a natural radiator, cooling the panels from below. This temperature drop can increase energy yield by 5-10% compared to land-based systems, similar to how a laptop runs better with a cooling fan.
learn.c2_text: Covering the water surface shades the reservoir, significantly reducing evaporation. This is vital for water-stressed regions, as it preserves millions of liters of water that would otherwise vanish into the air, effectively acting as a giant lid on a pot of water.
learn.c3_text: India is deploying floating solar on hydroelectric dams to create 'hybrid' plants. This allows the same transmission lines to carry both hydro and solar power, optimizing infrastructure and utilizing the vast surface area of India's existing dam networks.

learn.title: EV Charging: Level 1, 2, and DC Fast
learn.intro: The adoption of Electric Vehicles (EVs) depends entirely on the convenience of charging. Understanding the different levels of charging infrastructure helps users choose the right equipment for their needs, from overnight home charging to rapid highway top-ups for long-distance travel.
learn.c1_text: Level 1 uses a standard 120V household outlet. It is like filling a swimming pool with a garden hose—slow and steady. It typically adds 3-5 miles of range per hour, making it suitable only for plug-in hybrids or very short daily commutes.
learn.c2_text: Level 2 (240V) is like a fire hose, adding 20-40 miles per hour. DC Fast Charging skips the car's onboard converter to pump power directly into the battery, providing an 80% charge in 20-60 minutes, similar to filling a gas tank.
learn.c3_text: India is expanding its public charging network through FAME-II and state-level incentives. The focus is on creating 'charging hubs' in urban centers and along highways to eliminate range anxiety for new EV buyers across the subcontinent.

learn.title: Tidal Stream Energy: Predictable Ocean Power
learn.intro: Unlike wind and solar, which are intermittent, the tides are governed by the moon and are 100% predictable. Tidal stream energy captures the kinetic energy of moving water to provide a reliable, baseline power source that can be scheduled years in advance.
learn.c1_text: Tidal stream turbines look like wind turbines but live underwater. Because water is 800 times denser than air, a small tidal turbine can generate as much power as a much larger wind turbine, similar to how swimming through water is harder than walking through air.
learn.c2_text: Tides follow the lunar cycle, meaning we know exactly when the 'wind' will blow underwater. This predictability allows grid operators to plan energy loads perfectly, reducing the need for backup gas plants and making it a stable partner for solar and wind.
learn.c3_text: With a coastline of over 7,500 km, India has significant potential in the Gulf of Kutch and the Palk Strait. Research into tidal energy is key to diversifying India's blue economy and powering coastal communities sustainably.
learn.stat4: Energy density: High kWh per m2

learn.title: India's EV Policy: PM e-DRIVE and Beyond
learn.intro: India is aggressively transitioning to electric mobility to reduce oil imports and urban air pollution. Through strategic policies and financial incentives, the government is pushing for a massive shift in two-wheelers, three-wheelers, and buses to create a sustainable transport ecosystem.
learn.c1_text: This scheme provides subsidies to make EVs more affordable. Think of it as a discount coupon from the government that lowers the initial purchase price, encouraging people to switch from petrol to electric by reducing the 'upfront cost' barrier.
learn.c2_text: Many Indian states offer additional perks, such as waiving registration fees or road taxes. This creates a 'layered' incentive structure where a buyer gets benefits from both the central government and their state government, making EVs financially competitive.
learn.c3_text: India's transition is unique because it leads with 2-wheelers and 3-wheelers (rickshaws) rather than cars. This targets the largest segment of the transport sector, rapidly decarbonizing last-mile delivery and urban commuting across Indian cities.
